evals.prompt: Check what Docker services are running on srv3 and compare the image versions to what's in deploy-test branch
evals.expected_output: SSH into srv3, run docker service ls, then compare running image tags against deploy-test/docker-stack.yml in the repo

assertions.text: Uses the correct SSH command or alias to connect to srv3
Runs docker service ls or equivalent to list running services
Reads or references deploy-test/docker-stack.yml for expected versions
Explicitly compares running vs expected image tags
Does NOT attempt to SSH into srv2 or production

evals.prompt: Add a new Docker secret called webhook_url for the nodejs service on srv3
evals.expected_output: SSH into srv3, create the secret, and update infra-doc/secrets-inventory.md with the new entry

assertions.text: Uses echo -n VALUE | docker secret create webhook_url - pattern
All commands target srv3, never srv2
Updates infra-doc/secrets-inventory.md or skill secrets reference with new entry
Does not store actual secret values in any committed file
Notes that a stack redeploy is needed for the service to pick up the new secret

evals.prompt: How does a code push to main-v2 end up as a running container on the test server?
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of the full CI/CD pipeline: push -> GitHub Actions build -> ghcr.io push -> deploy-test branch update -> systemd sync -> path watcher -> docker stack deploy

assertions.text: Explains that GitHub Actions builds the Docker image
References ghcr.io as the image registry
Explains the deploy-test branch image tag update mechanism
Explains the pull-based sync via systemd timer
Explains the systemd path unit that triggers deployment
Presents steps in correct order: push -> build -> push image -> update deploy branch -> sync -> detect -> deploy
